_comment: File-major casualty reflow (#878): two identical Spearmen-vs-Infantry standoffs, side by side, staged fresh for this demo (not a copy of spear-standoff.json). The LEFT pair's Spearmen keeps the new default (file_major_reform: true): each soldier holds a persistent file assignment, so a casualty only closes the gap within its own column, leaving the survivors' rear jagged -- some files run deeper than others. The RIGHT pair's Spearmen explicitly opts out (file_major_reform: false, the historical behavior): the whole grid recomputes fresh from the live headcount every tick, so the rear reflows and re-centres uniformly regardless of which files actually took the losses. Both Infantry attackers march in and lock melee under their own enemy-AI order -- no scripted steps needed. Verified via dump-state.sh (soldiers_full.slots, the canonical per-file target grid, de-rotated into the unit's own local frame): both regiments take comparable losses (24 down to the mid-teens by tick 720 -- exact counts vary a soldier or two between local and CI runs, since combat RNG is chaotic over a clip this long; don't pin an exact count, only the qualitative shape below). The two modes' depth-SPREAD magnitude at any single tick looks similar in both (a mathematical necessity of laying out n soldiers across 7 files -- ranks_for's own partial-last-rank math forces a spread of about 1 either way, regardless of which soldiers survive), so that isn't the real tell. The real difference is WHICH file ends up the uniquely shallowest, once the deepest-file set narrows to one late in the clip: the LEFT (file-major) regiment's shallow file settles on a real flank column (NOT the centre) because that's genuinely where more of its soldiers died and holds that identity -- tied to actual casualty history. The RIGHT (row-major) regiment's shallow file is always the DEAD-CENTRE column instead, by construction of UnitFormation.block_slots' own centre-closing partial rank, regardless of which files' soldiers actually died.
